title.en: The Model Catalog: GPT, Phi, Llama & Choosing Wisely

description.en: Navigate Foundry's model catalog — OpenAI, Phi, Llama, Mistral and more — and learn a practical decision framework for picking the right model, deployment type, and license for each workload.

story_variants.en:
## One Catalog, Nineteen Hundred Models

Open the **Model Catalog** in the Azure AI Foundry portal and you face over 1,900 models: OpenAI's GPT-4o and o-series reasoning models, Microsoft's small Phi family, Meta's Llama line, Mistral, Cohere, DeepSeek, Stability AI image models, embedding models, and industry-specific models. The catalog is not a museum — every entry can be deployed, benchmarked, and wired into your project. The real skill is *choosing*, because model choice drives cost, latency, quality, and compliance for the life of your application.

> A model is not 'good' or 'bad' — it is a fit or a misfit for a specific workload, budget, and risk profile. The catalog gives you the facts; this lesson gives you the decision framework.

## The Major Families

| Family | Publisher | Sweet spot | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| GPT-4o / GPT-4.1 / o-series | OpenAI (via Azure OpenAI) | Complex reasoning, multimodal, flagship quality | Deployed as Azure OpenAI deployments; broadest tooling support |
| GPT-4o-mini class | OpenAI | High-volume, cost-sensitive chat and extraction | Often 10-30x cheaper per token than flagships |
| Phi (Phi-4 family) | Microsoft | Small-model efficiency: edge, latency-critical, fine-tuning | Open weights; strong reasoning for size |
| Llama 3.x | Meta | Open-weights customization, self-managed fine-tunes | Community license — read it for commercial terms |
| Mistral, Cohere, DeepSeek | Various | Specific strengths: multilingual, embeddings, reasoning value | Many offered as serverless pay-per-token |

## Three Ways a Catalog Model Reaches Production

How a model is *hosted* matters as much as which model it is:

- **Azure OpenAI deployments** — OpenAI models deployed into your Azure OpenAI resource. Data stays in your tenant boundary; supports Standard (pay-as-you-go), Provisioned (PTU), Global, and Batch deployment types.
- **Serverless API (pay-per-token)** — many partner models (Llama, Mistral, Cohere) deployable as a managed serverless endpoint. No GPU management, billed per token, quick to start. Microsoft brands these *standard deployments in Foundry Models*.
- **Managed compute** — the model's weights run on GPU VMs you provision. Full control (custom containers, fine-tuned weights), but you pay for the VMs whether or not tokens flow, and you own capacity planning.

> **Pitfall:** teams pick managed compute 'for control', then discover a mostly-idle A100 fleet on the invoice. Default to serverless or Azure OpenAI deployments; move to managed compute only when you genuinely need custom weights or containers.

## The Decision Framework

Work through these questions in order:

1. **Capability floor.** Does the task need frontier reasoning (multi-step analysis, tricky code)? If yes, shortlist flagship models. If it is classification, extraction, or routine chat, a small model likely clears the bar — *verify with evaluation, not vibes*.
2. **Latency and placement.** Sub-second responses or on-edge inference push you toward small models like Phi. Batch summarization tolerates slower, cheaper options (including Batch deployments at steep discounts).
3. **Cost at your volume.** A model that is slightly better but an order of magnitude pricier rarely wins at millions of requests per day. Estimate monthly tokens first; the next lesson covers PTU vs pay-as-you-go in depth.
4. **Customization.** Need to fine-tune or own the weights? Open-weights families (Phi, Llama, Mistral) are the natural home.
5. **Compliance and data boundary.** Regulated industries often require specific regions, tenant isolation, and content-filter guarantees — check the model's deployment options and region availability *before* committing.

## Benchmarks in the Catalog — Use, Don't Worship

The catalog surfaces benchmark comparisons (quality, cost, throughput) and model cards. These are excellent for *shortlisting* but public benchmarks saturate and rarely resemble your workload. The professional loop is:

- Shortlist 2-3 candidates from benchmarks and constraints.
- Build a small golden dataset from real traffic (even 50 examples).
- Run Foundry **evaluations** on all candidates (lesson 7).
- Pick with data; record the decision.

> Model choice is not permanent. The Foundry model router and your own abstraction layer can shift traffic as new models ship — design so a model swap is a config change, not a rewrite.

## Licensing and Lifecycle Realities

- **Licenses differ.** OpenAI models come under Microsoft's product terms; Llama uses Meta's community license; some models restrict certain use cases. Legal review is part of model selection in an enterprise.
- **Models retire.** Azure OpenAI publishes deprecation dates; a model version you deploy today will eventually be retired. Track retirement dates and bake upgrade testing into your roadmap.
- **Versions are pinned.** Deployments reference a model *version*. Auto-update policies exist, but regulated teams often pin versions and upgrade deliberately after re-running evaluations.

## A Worked Example

An insurer builds three features:

- **Claims-note summarizer** (2M documents/month, quality tolerant): a mini-class model on a Batch or Standard deployment. Flagship quality is wasted here.
- **Policy Q&A copilot** (grounded RAG, customer-facing): GPT-4o class for answer quality, paired with strict groundedness evaluation.
- **On-prem triage at branch offices** (data cannot leave site): Phi-4 small model, open weights, deployed to local hardware.

One product, three models — that heterogeneity is normal, and the catalog plus a consistent evaluation harness is what makes it manageable.

## Key Takeaways

- The catalog spans OpenAI, Phi, Llama, Mistral, Cohere, DeepSeek and more, with three hosting paths: Azure OpenAI deployments, serverless pay-per-token, managed compute.
- Choose by capability floor, latency, volume economics, customization needs, and compliance — in that order.
- Benchmarks shortlist; *your* evaluations decide.
- Plan for model retirement and make swapping models cheap.

description.en: Model families map to different hosting paths — the pairing is a design decision.

left.label: Pick the biggest model for everything and skip evaluation

right.label: Shortlist by constraints, then let your own eval dataset decide

description.en: Flagship-everywhere burns budget; evaluation-driven selection matches model to workload.

animation_sequence.code:
candidates = ['gpt-4o', 'gpt-4o-mini', 'phi-4']
for model in candidates:
    score = run_eval(model, golden_set)
    print(model, score)
best = max(candidates, key=lambda m: run_eval(m, golden_set))

explanation.en: Shortlist 2-3 candidates from catalog benchmarks and constraints.
Run the same evaluation harness against every candidate — identical data, identical metrics.
Selection becomes a reproducible computation instead of a debate.

lines: Decision: gpt-4o-mini clears the 0.90 quality floor at lowest cost

description.en: A capability floor plus cost ordering turns model choice into arithmetic.

instruction.en: Build a deterministic model-selection engine like an architect would sketch before touching the catalog. Implement select_model(task) where task is a dict with keys 'needs_reasoning' (bool), 'budget' ('low'/'medium'/'high'), 'latency' ('low'/'normal'), and 'open_weights' (bool). Rules in priority order: (1) needs_reasoning and budget 'high' -> 'gpt-4o'; (2) latency 'low' and budget 'low' -> 'phi-4'; (3) open_weights True -> 'llama-3.3-70b'; (4) otherwise 'gpt-4o-mini'. Apply it to the four tasks in the starter code and print one line per task in order, formatted exactly '<name> -> <model>'. This mirrors capability-floor-first selection from the lesson.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
def select_model(task):
    # TODO: implement the 4 priority rules from the instruction
    pass

tasks = [
    {'name': 'complex-analysis', 'needs_reasoning': True, 'budget': 'high', 'latency': 'normal', 'open_weights': False},
    {'name': 'edge-chatbot', 'needs_reasoning': False, 'budget': 'low', 'latency': 'low', 'open_weights': False},
    {'name': 'custom-finetune', 'needs_reasoning': False, 'budget': 'medium', 'latency': 'normal', 'open_weights': True},
    {'name': 'simple-faq', 'needs_reasoning': False, 'budget': 'low', 'latency': 'normal', 'open_weights': False},
]

for t in tasks:
    # TODO: print f"{t['name']} -> {select_model(t)}"
    pass
practice_challenges.expected_output:
complex-analysis -> gpt-4o
edge-chatbot -> phi-4
custom-finetune -> llama-3.3-70b
simple-faq -> gpt-4o-mini

en: Use an if/elif/elif/else chain — the ORDER of the rules is the whole point (capability floor first, then latency, then customization).
Rule 1: if task['needs_reasoning'] and task['budget'] == 'high': return 'gpt-4o'
The final else returns 'gpt-4o-mini'; print with f"{t['name']} -> {select_model(t)}".

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert select_model({'name': 'x', 'needs_reasoning': True, 'budget': 'high', 'latency': 'low', 'open_weights': True}) == 'gpt-4o'
assert select_model({'name': 'x', 'needs_reasoning': False, 'budget': 'low', 'latency': 'low', 'open_weights': True}) == 'phi-4'
assert select_model({'name': 'x', 'needs_reasoning': False, 'budget': 'high', 'latency': 'normal', 'open_weights': True}) == 'llama-3.3-70b'
assert select_model({'name': 'x', 'needs_reasoning': False, 'budget': 'medium', 'latency': 'normal', 'open_weights': False}) == 'gpt-4o-mini'

q.en: Which hosting path bills per token with no GPU management, common for partner models like Llama and Mistral?
What is the main financial risk of choosing managed compute hosting?

en: You pay for provisioned GPU VMs even when idle

q.en: Why should public catalog benchmarks not be the final word in model selection?

en: They rarely resemble your specific workload; your own eval dataset should decide
Benchmarks only exist for OpenAI models

q.en: A model version you deployed will eventually be retired by Azure OpenAI. What is the professional response?

en: Ignore it; deployments never stop working
Track retirement dates and re-run evaluations before upgrading versions
Switch to managed compute to avoid retirement
Delete the project and start over
